results.title: Multi-Agent Coordination Patterns: Architectures Beyond the Hype | by Oleksandr Husiev | Medium
excerpts:
Section Title: ... > Blackboard: The Shared Brain
Content:
Still, if your goal is shared intelligence over speed, blackboard is one of the most powerful metaphors — and it’s already working quietly behind the scenes in many modern agent stacks.
 ... 
Section Title: ... > Swarm: Decentralized Emergence
Content:
Swarm-based coordination abandons central planning altogether. Agents follow local rules, sense their neighborhood, and act accordingly. Over time, these small actions compound into global behavior.
Nature is full of such systems — ant colonies, bird flocks, immune systems. In software, they show up in agent-based simulations and decentralized monitoring systems.
Swarm logic is powerful, but not magic. Without incentives or boundaries, it often leads to noise, not signal. And with LLM agents, the challenges amplify — token cost, context loss, lack of shared history all weaken the cohesion.
Still, when scale matters — 1000+ agents monitoring real-time signals, adapting to novel threats, or exploring large search spaces — swarms are the only viable path. But be warned: you don’t control them, you just shape the environment.
Section Title: Multi-Agent Coordination Patterns: Architectures Beyond the Hype > ... > Choosing the Right Pattern
Content:
These aren’t mutually exclusive strategies. In practice, real systems blend them. A swarm might publish updates to a blackboard. A marketplace might be implemented as a Pub/Sub layer with auction topics. Agents don’t care about metaphors — they care about bandwidth, latency, and memory access.
If you want tight collaboration and global visibility, start with a blackboard.
If you want resilience and simple plug-and-play behaviors, go with Pub/Sub.
If you want autonomy and planning, use marketplace logic.
And if you’re scaling fast into unknown territory, swarm is your only real option.
Architecture isn’t about picking one tool — it’s about composing them with constraints in mind.

References (72)
Section Title: ... > Abstract
Content:
The convergence of hierarchical swarm intelligence with artificial intelligence systems represents a transformative paradigm in autonomous vehicle network management, addressing critical challenges in coordination, decision-making, and collective behavior optimization. This comprehensive research review examines the integration mechanisms, algorithmic frameworks, and operational strategies that enable scalable autonomous vehicle coordination through multi-level swarm intelligence architectures. By analyzing the intersection of distributed intelligence, hierarchical control systems, and machine learning algorithms, this study reveals how autonomous vehicle networks can achieve emergent collective behavior while maintaining individual vehicle autonomy and safety requirements.
 ...
